anonymized_passage: after whether local adaptation improves fit or conceals self-serving behavior has been fully observed, a multiunit firm's senior team chooses how much discretion to delegate using unit conditions, coordination needs, and observable performance signals. The stated objective is to balance local responsiveness with cross-unit coordination, subject to the condition that headquarters cannot directly observe every local motive or consequence. Delegation improves local fit while common rules limit coordination and agency losses. Greater discretion supports adaptation but weakens consistency and oversight. The theory concerns multiunit settings with partial observability and does not imply that decentralization always improves performance. The study explains delegation as a response to joint adaptation and observability conditions rather than as a generic best practice.

model_or_study_facts: headquarters cannot directly observe every local motive or consequence
whether local adaptation improves fit or conceals self-serving behavior
decisions: chooses how much discretion to delegate
context.objective: balance local responsiveness with cross-unit coordination
context.timing: when local units propose exceptions to a common policy
decision_time_information: unit conditions, coordination needs, and observable performance signals
constraints: headquarters cannot directly observe every local motive or consequence
context.mechanism: Delegation improves local fit while common rules limit coordination and agency losses
supplied_evidence: Synthetic illustration; no external empirical evidence supplied.
